title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Marine
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: GPS spoofing and AIS jamming scramble more than 1,100 ships around the Strait of Hormuz
items.content_text: Maritime-intelligence firms including Windward documented GNSS interference affecting over 1,100 vessels across the Middle East Gulf in a single 24-hour window as hostilities among Iran, the U.S. and Israel escalated. Ships’ reported positions were displaced onto airports, inland sites and even a nuclear plant; Windward flagged at least 21 new AIS jamming clusters across UAE, Qatari, Omani and Iranian waters, and Hormuz transits fell roughly a third below normal. For vessel and terminal operators the lesson is concrete: in contested waters, satellite position, timing and AIS tracks cannot be treated as authoritative. Spoofed positions raise collision risk in congested lanes and can trigger false compliance and sanctions-screening alerts, while switching AIS off to avoid mislocation sacrifices collision-avoidance visibility — pushing reliance back onto radar, inertial aids and disciplined visual watchkeeping.

items.title: King Ocean Services discloses a data breach at a Florida-based container shipping line
items.content_text: King Ocean Services, a Florida-headquartered ocean-freight and container carrier running services across the Western Hemisphere, began notifying individuals on 11 July of a data-security incident that exposed personal information, including government identification numbers. The company has not disclosed the cause, the intrusion timeline, or whether any operational or terminal systems were touched — the filings describe a data-theft/PII exposure, not a confirmed impact to cargo or vessel OT. It is logged here as a reminder that shipping lines and their shoreside back-office systems are being hit directly, and that an IT compromise at a carrier can ripple into booking, manifest and terminal-handoff processes even when navigation and control systems stay untouched. Confirmed via the company’s own breach notice; operational details remain limited.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: King Ocean Services breach notice / Cole & Van Note / ISSSource

items.title: Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the bridges between shipboard serial gear and the network
items.content_text: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038 in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, one of the flaws disclosed in April through the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. This category deserves particular attention at sea and on the quayside. Maritime OT is still overwhelmingly serial at the sensor and actuator layer — NMEA feeds, engine and auxiliary monitoring, alarm and monitoring panels, cargo and ballast systems, crane and gate equipment — and serial-to-IP converters are what connected all of it to shipboard or terminal Ethernet during retrofits and digitalisation projects. They are typically installed by an integrator, absent from any drawing, and never patched because the equipment behind them still works. Exploitation is confirmed and follows an explicit OT threat warning; for most operators the first task is establishing what is actually installed.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Disclosed Apr 2026 — exploitation reported 25 Jun 2026; ongoing

items.title: USCG Cyber Command’s fifth CTIME report: reported maritime cyber incidents up 17%, phishing dominant
items.content_text: Coast Guard Cyber Command (CGCYBER) released its fifth annual Cyber Trends and Insights in the Marine Environment (CTIME) report, drawing on a record 42 Cyber Protection Team missions plus industry telemetry from 2025. Reported maritime cyber incidents rose 17% year-over-year. Phishing was the initial-access vector in 43% of incidents — an 18-point jump — with syndicates such as Scattered Spider pairing phishing with voice impersonation (vishing) to defeat help desks and weak MFA; ransomware featured in 19% of attack paths, and OT was in scope for 62% of the command’s missions. CGCYBER stresses that the blurring IT/OT boundary aboard ships, machinery and port facilities raises the odds of a cyber event producing physical consequences. Notably, Cyber Protection Teams deployed alongside boarding teams in Dark Fleet interdiction operations for the first time.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Fifth annual CTIME report — released Jul 2026 (2025 data)

items.title: SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days under active attack; both added to CISA KEV
items.content_text: SonicWall confirmed active exploitation of two Secure Mobile Access 1000 flaws after investigating multiple cases. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface; CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2) lets an authenticated administrator inject OS commands through the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July with a 17 July federal deadline. For ports, terminals and vessel operators the placement is what matters: this class of appliance brokers the shoreside and vendor access that reaches terminal operating systems, crane and gate control, and satcom-backhauled vessel networks. A confirmed compromise is also a reportable-incident question under the USCG rule, where notification to the National Response Center is due immediately on discovery — a notification decision, not just a patch.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Advisory & KEV addition 14 Jul 2026 — exploitation ongoing

items.title: July ICS Patch Tuesday: Siemens ships a CVSS 10 auth bypass fix; Rockwell patches critical controller DoS flaws
items.content_text: The July 2026 cycle matters to terminals and shipyards running mainstream industrial automation. Siemens published nine advisories, six critical — headlined by a CVSS 10 token-invalidation flaw in Opencenter X allowing authentication bypass and full application access, with further critical fixes in Simatic S7-1500, Mendix, Sidis Secured SmartPlug, Cadra and Desigo CC (the last being building-management software common in terminal and port facilities). Rockwell published 12 advisories including three critical denial-of-service holes in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers that can force a major non-recoverable fault, plus a critical unauthenticated CLI exposure in 1715 Redundant I/O. Schneider fixed a high-severity code-execution flaw in IGSS SCADA. VDE CERT added five advisories covering Codesys and Wago among others.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: Siemens ProductCERT / Schneider / Rockwell PSIRT / SecurityWeek

items.title: BeyondTrust patches critical auth-bypass flaws in the privileged remote access tooling vendors use
items.content_text: BeyondTrust disclosed two authentication-bypass vulnerabilities on 7 July in Remote Support (RS) and Privileged Remote Access (PRA). CVE-2026-40138 (CVSS 9.2) stems from improper validation of authentication data and could let a network-positioned attacker bypass access controls and reach the appliance, including elevated accounts; CVE-2026-40139 involves improper processing of RS authentication requests. Both require a specific authentication configuration to be enabled, which limits exposure, and neither is under active exploitation — no public proof-of-concept exists. Cloud customers were patched on 21 April; self-hosted RS/PRA at 25.3.2 or earlier needs action. Alongside the SonicWall top story, the pattern is clear enough: vendor remote maintenance — the standing route OEMs use into vessel and terminal systems — is under sustained research attention.

items.title: CISA advisories cover Digi PortServer TS and Digi One SP IA9 serial device servers
items.content_text: CISA released seven ICS advisories on 7 July, among them Digi International PortServer TS and Digi One SP IA9 — serial device servers from the same family as the Lantronix converters currently being exploited (see Incidents). The rest of the batch covers Hitachi Energy PROMOD V and e-mesh EMS, Siemens Mendix Studio Pro and SINEC OS, Labcenter Proteus 9 and the Hydro-Québec Le Circuit Électrique charging-station backend; those are energy- and water-flagged rather than maritime. The Digi entries are the maritime-relevant part, and worth reading together with the Lantronix exploitation: two advisories against the humble serial-server category inside a fortnight suggests researchers are looking hard at it, which has historically preceded attackers doing the same.

items.title: CISA’s 14 July batch carries three ABB advisories — worth a look given ABB’s marine automation footprint
items.content_text: CISA issued four ICS advisories on 14 July (ICSA-26-195-01 through -04), released alongside vendor Patch Tuesday advisories: three from ABB and one from Rockwell. The ABB entries cover Advant Master Online Builder, where an incorrect Online Builder version was included on installation media, and Ability Edgenius, where a Linux kernel privilege-escalation issue could let a locally authenticated user or a compromised container workload gain root. To be precise about scope: these are ABB industrial products, not ABB Marine systems, so they do not name shipboard equipment. They are flagged here because ABB is a significant supplier into marine propulsion, power and automation, and operators with mixed ABB estates ashore — at terminals, yards and support facilities — should check the affected version lists rather than assume the advisories miss them.

items.title: The USCG cyber rule’s big deadline is 16 July 2027: Cybersecurity Officer, assessment and plan
items.content_text: A forward-looking compliance marker rather than news. Under the Coast Guard’s Cybersecurity in the Marine Transportation System final rule, U.S.-flagged vessels, OCS facilities and waterfront facilities must, by 16 July 2027, designate a Cybersecurity Officer, complete an initial cybersecurity assessment (annually thereafter) and submit a Cybersecurity Plan to the USCG for approval. Two earlier milestones are already live and worth confirming you meet: reportable cyber incidents have had to go to the National Response Center immediately on discovery since 16 July 2025, and the personnel cybersecurity training requirement took effect 16 January 2026. The Coast Guard has also signalled that Port State Control scrutiny will extend to indicators of poor cybersecurity practice on foreign-flagged vessels, with deficiencies, detention or denial of entry available as outcomes.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Final rule effective 16 Jul 2025; training due 16 Jan 2026; CySO, assessment & plan due 16 Jul 2027
